behaviors.description: A file placed inside a subdirectory of the watched root is processed, not only files placed directly in the root directory.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is real filesystem watch behavior (recursive subscription and event delivery for nested paths). In-process mocks can miss OS/backend watcher differences, so REAL-INFRA and MOCK-HIDE both push to System.
behaviors.test_description: Start the real daemon against a temp root directory on disk, create a nested subdirectory, write a file there, and assert exactly one transformed output is produced for that nested file path.

behaviors.description: The deduplication logic correctly discards a second processing request for the same file path within a configurable time window.
behaviors.justification: Behavior text targets dedup logic itself (same path + time window), which is a local state/time rule and does not require OS/network/subprocess. Higher levels were considered and rejected because the behavior does not require real filesystem event semantics, only request-level dedup correctness.
behaviors.test_description: Call the dedup component/function with the same file path twice inside the configured window (using a fake clock), assert first request is accepted and second is rejected; then advance beyond window and assert acceptance again.

behaviors.description: When the operating system emits multiple filesystem events for a single file write, the daemon processes the file exactly once.
behaviors.justification: Behavior explicitly depends on OS-emitted duplicate filesystem events. This is a real infrastructure condition; mocks can simulate duplicates but may not reproduce backend-specific timing/event patterns that cause real duplicate processing bugs.
behaviors.test_description: Run daemon against a real temp directory, perform a write pattern known to trigger multiple events (e.g., write+rename/chunked append), capture processing invocations, and assert exactly one processing/output for the target file.

behaviors.description: If the watched directory is removed and then recreated while the daemon is running, the daemon resumes watching the new directory without a restart.
behaviors.justification: This is OS/filesystem lifecycle behavior (delete/recreate inode/path and watcher reattachment). In-process fakes typically hide failures around handle invalidation and re-subscription after real directory replacement.
behaviors.test_description: Start daemon watching a real directory, delete the directory while running, recreate it at the same path, then add a new file and assert it is processed without restarting the daemon.

behaviors.description: When multiple files arrive at the same time, they are processed in the order determined by their creation timestamps.
behaviors.justification: Primary failure boundary is handoff/order logic across watcher ingest -> queue -> scheduler (cross-module state/order propagation), matching the integration-handoff escalator. System was considered and rejected because behavior text requires ordering by timestamps, not specifically OS-specific event delivery quirks; this can be validated with in-process modules plus controlled file metadata fixtures.
behaviors.test_description: Wire watcher-event ingestion, metadata lookup abstraction, and processing queue together in-process; inject simultaneous arrivals with controlled creation timestamps and assert processing order matches ascending configured timestamp policy.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: If implementation reads raw OS birthtime directly without abstraction, add a System test for platform timestamp resolution ties.

behaviors.description: A file that is still being written to when the arrival event fires is not picked up for processing until writing is complete.
behaviors.justification: Correctness depends on real filesystem write-in-progress timing and event ordering. Mocked writes often appear atomic and can hide races between watcher notification and file completeness checks.
behaviors.test_description: With daemon running on a real directory, create a file via slow/chunked write, verify processing does not start during partial writes, finalize/close the file, then assert processing starts and output matches full content.
